real_meaning: This pattern actually covers TWO distinct usages that sound identical but mean very different things. The first is <ruby>様子<rt>ようす</rt></ruby>そうだ (appearance/conjecture): 'it looks like / it seems like' — based on direct visual or sensory evidence RIGHT NOW in front of you. The second is <ruby>伝聞<rt>でんぶん</rt></ruby>そうだ (hearsay): 'I heard that / apparently' — reporting something you learned from an outside source like news or gossip. Context and verb form are the only way to tell them apart, which is what makes this pattern genuinely tricky.

formation.rule: APPEARANCE (looks like): attach そうだ to the STEM of い-adjectives (drop い → <ruby>高<rt>たか</rt></ruby>い → <ruby>高<rt>たか</rt></ruby>そうだ), stem of な-adjectives (drop だ → <ruby>元気<rt>げんき</rt></ruby>そうだ), and the masu-stem of verbs (<ruby>降<rt>ふ</rt></ruby>る → <ruby>降<rt>ふ</rt></ruby>りそうだ). HEARSAY (I heard that): attach そうだ to the PLAIN/DICTIONARY form of verbs (<ruby>降<rt>ふ</rt></ruby>る → <ruby>降<rt>ふ</rt></ruby>るそうだ), plain form adjectives (<ruby>高<rt>たか</rt></ruby>いそうだ), and noun+だ (<ruby>学生<rt>がくせい</rt></ruby>だそうだ).
formation.common_mistake: Learners mix up the two by using dictionary form for appearance: ✗ <ruby>雨<rt>あめ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>降<rt>ふ</rt></ruby>るそうだ when trying to say 'it looks like it will rain' (this actually means 'I heard it will rain'). The correct appearance form is ✓ <ruby>雨<rt>あめ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>降<rt>ふ</rt></ruby>りそうだ (masu-stem + そうだ).

sentences.jp: この<ruby>ケーキ<rt>けーき</rt></ruby>、すごくおいしそう！
sentences.notes: Classic appearance そうだ — the speaker is looking directly at the cake right now and making a sensory judgment; note い is dropped from おいしい before そう.

sentences.jp: <ruby>天気予報<rt>てんきよほう</rt></ruby>によると、<ruby>明日<rt>あした</rt></ruby>は<ruby>雪<rt>ゆき</rt></ruby>が<ruby>降<rt>ふ</rt></ruby>るそうです。
sentences.translation: According to the weather forecast, it will apparently snow tomorrow.
sentences.notes: Hearsay そうだ — the speaker learned this from a third-party source (the forecast), not from their own senses; dictionary form 降る precedes そうです.

sentences.jp: <ruby>部長<rt>ぶちょう</rt></ruby>が<ruby>来月<rt>らいげつ</rt></ruby><ruby>退職<rt>たいしょく</rt></ruby>されるそうでございます。
sentences.translation: I have heard that the department head will be retiring next month.
sentences.notes: Hearsay そうだ in a very formal register, layered with honorific ～される and ございます, as you might say when relaying information to a superior.

sentences.jp: <ruby>彼女<rt>かのじょ</rt></ruby>は<ruby>泣<rt>な</rt></ruby>きそうな<ruby>顔<rt>かお</rt></ruby>をしていたけど、<ruby>泣<rt>な</rt></ruby>かなかった。
sentences.translation: She had a face that looked like she was about to cry, but she didn't.
sentences.notes: Tricky use: そうな modifies the noun 顔, showing that appearance そうだ can act as an adjective — many learners don't realize そうだ has a prenominal そうな form.

confused_with.contrast: Both ～そうだ (hearsay) and ～らしい can translate as 'apparently' or 'I heard that,' but they differ in how solid the evidence feels. ～らしい implies the speaker has gathered some indirect evidence or inference and is drawing a reasonable conclusion, while hearsay ～そうだ simply reports something heard from a specific source with no personal judgment added. Compare: <ruby>田中<rt>たなか</rt></ruby>さんは<ruby>結婚<rt>けっこん</rt></ruby>したそうだ ('I heard Tanaka got married' — directly relaying what someone told you) vs. <ruby>田中<rt>たなか</rt></ruby>さんは<ruby>結婚<rt>けっこん</rt></ruby>したらしい ('It seems Tanaka got married' — you've picked up various clues and it all points that way). Choose そうだ when you can cite a clear source; choose らしい when it's more of an impression built from indirect evidence.

bunpro_tip: Use this mental split to nail BunPro drills: STEM (masu-stem or adjective stem) + そうだ = 'looks like' (your EYES are the source), PLAIN form + そうだ = 'I heard that' (someone ELSE is the source). When you see the full dictionary/plain form right before そうだ, ask yourself 'could I have witnessed this directly?' — if no, it's hearsay and you're on the right track.
